deductions.reason: Across all three conversations, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—why the Socratic approach supports learning, retention, or skill development. The tutor enforces constraints (e.g., 'No—I won't fill in a row for you') but does not articulate the pedagogical rationale behind this stance.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turn 8; Transcript 2, Turn 8; Transcript 3, Turn 8-10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful improvements (e.g., T2 student drafts a solid justification in Turn 8; T3 student produces good air pollution reasoning in Turn 9), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice, consistently refusing to give direct answers, scaffolding student reasoning step by step, and keeping key idea generation student-led. The tutor builds well on student progress and avoids redundancy. Two consistent gaps appear: the tutor never explains the pedagogical rationale for its guiding approach, and it misses opportunities to prompt reflection after student improvement. All other criteria are met at a high level.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to derive conclusions before offering any framing. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on student progress across turns and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained (1.3.A.a triggered across all transcripts—the Turn 3 refusal in T1 is the closest but gives no learning rationale), and after clear student improvement moments the tutor moves forward without prompting process reflection (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong, with no evidence of repetition without progress, assignment drift, monologue-style responses, or inappropriate tone.
